modules.title: Hacking Wireless Networks
modules.description: Comprehensive textbook-level coverage of wireless networking standards, encryption protocols, and advanced wireless attack methodologies including evil twin attacks, KRACK, Bluetooth exploitation, and RFID cloning.
objectives: Understand the intricate details of wireless standards (802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax/be) and RF principles.
Analyze wireless authentication and encryption (WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3).
Execute advanced attacks such as KRACK, WPS Pixie Dust, and Deauthentication.
Perform Bluetooth, RFID, and NFC exploitation.
Utilize enterprise-grade auditing tools like Aircrack-ng, Kismet, and WiFi Pineapple.

topics.title: Wireless Concepts and Topologies
topics.content:
Introduction to Wireless Networks

Wireless Local Area Networks (WLANs) transmit data over the air using Radio Frequency (RF) waves. They provide flexibility but inherently lack physical boundaries, meaning anyone within range can intercept the signals.

WLAN Topologies

  • BSS (Basic Service Set): A single Access Point (AP) connecting multiple wireless clients. It is identified by a BSSID, which is typically the MAC address of the AP's wireless interface.
  • ESS (Extended Service Set): Multiple interconnected APs sharing the same SSID (Service Set Identifier), allowing clients to roam seamlessly across a larger area.
  • IBSS (Independent BSS) / Ad-hoc: A peer-to-peer network where clients communicate directly without a central AP.

RF Principles

Wireless communication depends on frequency, wavelength, and amplitude. The 2.4 GHz band offers longer range but is susceptible to interference (microwaves, Bluetooth), while the 5 GHz band provides higher throughput with shorter range. Antennas (omnidirectional for general coverage, directional like Yagi for targeted range) dictate signal propagation.

topics.title: 802.11 Standards Deep Dive
topics.content:
The IEEE 802.11 Family

The 802.11 standards define the Physical (PHY) and MAC layers for WLANs.

  • 802.11a/b/g: Legacy standards. 802.11b (11 Mbps) and 802.11g (54 Mbps) operate in 2.4 GHz. 802.11a (54 Mbps) operates in 5 GHz.
  • 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4): Introduced MIMO (Multiple Input Multiple Output), using multiple antennas to transmit independent data streams, significantly boosting throughput (up to 600 Mbps). Operates in both 2.4 and 5 GHz.
  • 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5): Operates strictly in 5 GHz. Introduced wider channel bandwidths (up to 160 MHz) and MU-MIMO (Multi-User MIMO) for downlink traffic.
  • 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6): Focuses on high efficiency in dense environments. Introduces OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency-Division Multiple Access), allowing APs to communicate with multiple devices simultaneously on sub-channels.
  • 802.11be (Wi-Fi 7): The latest generation, supporting 320 MHz channels and 4K-QAM, aiming for Multi-Link Operation (MLO) across 2.4, 5, and 6 GHz bands simultaneously.

topics.title: Wireless Authentication and Encryption
topics.content:
Open System vs. Shared Key Authentication

In the original 802.11 specification, clients authenticated using Open System (no authentication) or Shared Key (requires a WEP key). Ironically, Shared Key authentication reveals plaintext/ciphertext pairs, making it weaker than Open System combined with encryption.

Evolution of Wireless Security

  • WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy): The first standard, utilizing the RC4 stream cipher and a 24-bit Initialization Vector (IV). Due to the small IV space, IVs are reused, allowing attackers to collect frames and statistically derive the key. Completely deprecated.
  • WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access): An interim solution utilizing TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol). TKIP dynamically changes keys per packet, preventing IV reuse attacks. However, since it still uses RC4, it remains vulnerable to attacks like ChopChop.
  • WPA2: Mandates the use of AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) in CCMP (Counter Mode CBC-MAC Protocol). AES-CCMP provides robust confidentiality and integrity. The primary vulnerability is the 4-way handshake PSK (Pre-Shared Key) exchange.
  • WPA3: Introduces SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals), replacing the PSK handshake and eliminating offline dictionary attacks.

topics.title: WEP and WPA Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
WEP Exploitation

WEP cracking relies on collecting sufficient IVs. Attackers use tools like aireplay-ng to inject packets (e.g., ARP Request Replay) into the network, forcing the AP to rapidly generate new IVs. Once 10,000 to 50,000 IVs are captured by airodump-ng, aircrack-ng uses algorithms (like PTW or FMS/Korek) to compute the static WEP key in seconds.

WPA/TKIP Attacks

While TKIP solved WEP's IV problem, it retained RC4 and introduced a weak Message Integrity Check (MIC) called Michael. The ChopChop attack allows an attacker to interactively guess the last byte of a packet based on the AP's MIC failure reports. The Beck-Tews attack exploits QoS (Quality of Service) features to inject limited packets into a WPA-TKIP network.

topics.title: WPA2 and The 4-Way Handshake
topics.content:
The WPA2 4-Way Handshake

In WPA2-Personal, the AP and client must establish a Pairwise Transient Key (PTK) for session encryption. This is done via the 4-way handshake:

  1. AP sends an ANonce (Authenticator Nonce) to the client.
  2. Client computes the PTK (derived from the PSK, ANonce, SNonce, AP MAC, and Client MAC). Client sends the SNonce (Supplicant Nonce) and a MIC to the AP.
  3. AP verifies the MIC, computes its own PTK, and sends the GTK (Group Temporal Key for broadcast traffic) with a MIC.
  4. Client acknowledges (ACK) the installation of the keys.

Offline Dictionary Attacks

An attacker can force a client to disconnect (Deauth attack). When the client reconnects, the attacker captures the 4-way handshake using airodump-ng. The attacker then uses hashcat or aircrack-ng to compute the PTK for every password in a dictionary. If the computed MIC matches the captured MIC, the password is cracked. This is purely an offline attack against weak passwords.

topics.title: KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attacks)
topics.content:
Understanding KRACK

Discovered in 2017, KRACK represents a fundamental flaw in the 802.11 standard itself, specifically the WPA2 4-way handshake. The vulnerability lies in how the standard handles dropped messages. If the AP's Message 3 is lost, the AP retransmits it. The client, receiving Message 3 again, is forced to reinstall the PTK.

The Exploit Mechanism

An attacker in a Man-in-the-Middle position blocks Message 4 (the ACK) from reaching the AP. The AP then retransmits Message 3. The client receives the retransmitted Message 3 and reinstalls the same encryption key. Critically, doing so resets the packet sequence number (nonce) to zero. Nonce reuse in AES-CCMP or TKIP allows the attacker to derive the keystream, decrypt arbitrary packets, and potentially forge traffic (like injecting malicious JavaScript into HTTP streams). The fix required patching the client-side state machine across all OS vendors.

topics.title: WPS (Wi-Fi Protected Setup) Exploitation
topics.content:
WPS Architecture Flaws

WPS was created to simplify connecting devices using an 8-digit PIN printed on the router. However, the PIN validation protocol is deeply flawed. The AP authenticates the first 4 digits separately from the last 4 digits (which includes a checksum). This reduces the cryptographic search space from 100,000,000 (10^8) combinations to just 11,000 (10^4 + 10^3).

WPS Attacks

  • Brute Force (Reaver/Bully): Tools like Reaver sequentially guess the PIN. Due to AP rate-limiting, this can take hours or days, but it mathematically guarantees recovery of the WPA2 plaintext passphrase.
  • Pixie Dust Attack: An offline brute-force attack against WPS. It exploits poor randomization (weak entropy) in some router chipsets when generating the E-S1 and E-S2 nonces. By capturing a single WPS exchange, the attacker can crack the PIN offline in seconds.

Mitigation strictly requires disabling WPS completely.

topics.title: Rogue APs, Evil Twins, and Karma
topics.content:
Rogue Access Points

A rogue AP is an unauthorized AP connected to the corporate LAN. It bypasses network security controls (like 802.1X port security) and provides an unmonitored backdoor. They are detected via physical surveys or Wireless IDS (WIDS).

Evil Twin Attacks

An Evil Twin is a malicious AP created by an attacker to impersonate a legitimate network, using the same SSID. By emitting a stronger signal, clients automatically roam to the attacker's AP, enabling full Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) capabilities.

Karma Attacks and WiFi Pineapple

A Karma attack listens for 802.11 Probe Requests sent by clients searching for previously saved networks. The attacker's device (e.g., a Hak5 WiFi Pineapple) automatically replies with a Probe Response, impersonating whichever network the client is looking for (e.g., "Starbucks_WiFi"). Once connected, the attacker serves a Captive Portal—a fake login page mimicking a corporate portal or firmware update to harvest credentials.

topics.title: Deauthentication and Disassociation
topics.content:
802.11 Management Frames

In standard 802.11, Management Frames (like Deauth, Disassoc, Beacons) are transmitted in plaintext and are unauthenticated. This is a massive design flaw.

The Deauth Attack

An attacker can spoof the MAC address of an AP and send a Deauthentication frame to a connected client. The client trusts the frame and instantly disconnects. This is used for:

  • Targeted DoS: Keeping a specific client (like a security camera) offline continuously.
  • Handshake Capture: Forcing a client to disconnect and reconnect, allowing the attacker to capture the 4-way handshake.
  • Client Steering: Forcing clients off a legitimate AP so they connect to the attacker's Evil Twin.

802.11w (Management Frame Protection - MFP): Introduced to solve this by cryptographically protecting management frames, preventing spoofed deauths.

topics.title: Bluetooth and WPAN Exploitation
topics.content:
Bluetooth Architecture

Bluetooth is a WPAN (Wireless Personal Area Network) protocol operating in the 2.4 GHz band using Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS) to avoid interference. Security is heavily reliant on the pairing process and link keys.

Bluetooth Attacks

  • Bluejacking: Harmlessly sending unsolicited vCards or messages to discoverable devices. Used for reconnaissance.
  • Bluesnarfing: Exploiting OBEX (Object Exchange) protocol vulnerabilities to steal data (contacts, emails) without the user's knowledge.
  • Bluebugging: Gaining backdoor access and taking full control of the device's AT command set, allowing the attacker to make calls, send texts, and eavesdrop.
  • BlueBorne: A devastating 2017 vulnerability in the Bluetooth stack of Android, iOS, Windows, and Linux. It required zero user interaction, no pairing, and allowed Remote Code Execution (RCE) just by the device having Bluetooth enabled.

topics.title: RFID and NFC Attacks
topics.content:
RFID (Radio Frequency Identification)

Used widely for access control (office badges) and inventory. Legacy systems (like 125 kHz HID Prox) broadcast their facility code and card number in plaintext when energized by the reader's magnetic field.

  • RFID Cloning: Attackers use devices like the Proxmark3 or Flipper Zero to energize a victim's badge in their pocket, read the data, and clone it onto a blank T5577 card, completely bypassing physical building security.

NFC (Near Field Communication)

A high-frequency (13.56 MHz) subset of RFID used for Apple Pay, Google Wallet, and smart cards.

  • NFC Relay Attack: Two attackers work together. Attacker A holds a reader near the victim's NFC card. Attacker B holds a phone near the payment terminal. Attacker A relays the cryptographic challenge/response over the internet to Attacker B, completing the transaction even though the victim is far away.

topics.title: Wireless Reconnaissance and Aircrack-ng
topics.content:
Wardriving and Passive Reconnaissance

Wardriving involves physically moving through an area with GPS, antennas, and software like NetStumbler or Kismet to map wireless networks. The data is often uploaded to WiGLE (Wireless Geographic Logging Engine) to create global heatmaps of APs and their security types.

The Aircrack-ng Suite

Aircrack-ng is the standard toolset for 802.11 auditing.

  • airmon-ng: Sets the wireless interface into Monitor Mode, allowing it to capture all RF traffic on the channel without associating with an AP.
  • airodump-ng: A packet sniffer used to capture 802.11 frames, identify target APs, list connected clients, and capture handshakes (.cap files).
  • aireplay-ng: Performs packet injection. Used for Deauth attacks, Fake Authentication, and ARP Request Replays to generate traffic.
  • aircrack-ng: The cryptographic cracking engine. Performs statistical cracking (WEP) and dictionary attacks (WPA/WPA2) against captured handshakes.

modules.title: Hacking Mobile Platforms
modules.description: In-depth exploration of mobile application security, Android and iOS architectures, dynamic instrumentation with Frida, MDM bypass, and advanced network evasion techniques.
objectives: Analyze Android and iOS security models, including sandboxing, code signing, and IPC.
Execute dynamic and static analysis of mobile apps to identify OWASP Mobile Top 10 vulnerabilities.
Understand the implications of rooting, jailbreaking, and enterprise MDM bypass.
Perform dynamic instrumentation using Frida to bypass certificate pinning and root detection.

topics.title: Mobile OS Security Models
topics.content:
Android Architecture

Android is built on a customized Linux kernel. Its primary security mechanism is the Application Sandbox. Upon installation, each Android app receives a unique Linux User ID (UID). The kernel enforces process isolation, preventing apps from accessing each other's memory or storage. Inter-Process Communication (IPC) is strictly handled via Intents, Content Providers, and Broadcast Receivers, requiring explicit permissions declared in the AndroidManifest.xml.

iOS Architecture

iOS relies on a heavily restricted, multi-layered Unix core (Darwin). The hardware root of trust begins with the Secure Enclave. The iOS Sandbox (enforced by TrustedBSD/Seatbelt) strictly isolates apps into their own containers. Crucially, iOS mandates absolute Code Signing. The kernel checks the Apple-issued cryptographic signature of every executable before running it, practically eliminating the execution of dynamically injected, unsigned code unless the device is jailbroken.

topics.title: Rooting and Jailbreaking
topics.content:
Rooting Android

Rooting grants superuser access to the Android OS. It typically requires unlocking the bootloader via fastboot, flashing a custom recovery image (like TWRP), and installing a root management binary like su. Modern rooting utilizes Magisk, which employs a 'systemless' approach. Magisk modifies the boot image in RAM rather than altering the system partition, allowing it to evade basic root detection mechanisms like Google's SafetyNet or Play Integrity API.

Jailbreaking iOS

Jailbreaking removes Apple's software restrictions. It requires exploiting vulnerabilities (often zero-days) in the iOS kernel or bootrom to patch code-signing enforcement and mount the root filesystem as read-write. Types of jailbreaks include Tethered (requires a PC to boot), Untethered (persists across reboots), and Semi-untethered (requires running an app on the device after reboot). Tools like Cydia or Sileo are then installed as alternative app managers.

topics.title: Mobile Device Management (MDM) Bypass
topics.content:
Enterprise MDM Risk

Enterprises use MDM (like Intune or Workspace ONE) to enforce security policies, push certificates, and enable remote wipe capabilities. When a device is rooted or jailbroken, the fundamental security assumptions of the OS collapse. The application sandbox is broken, allowing malware to read corporate emails or MFA tokens from other apps.

MDM Evasion Techniques

An attacker (or rogue employee) with root access can completely bypass MDM controls. They can kill the MDM daemon, modify the configuration profiles stored on the device, or hook into the MDM application's API calls (using Frida) to falsely report the device state as 'compliant' and 'unrooted' back to the enterprise server, allowing a compromised device unfettered access to the corporate network.

topics.title: OWASP Mobile Top 10: Credentials and Storage
topics.content:
M1: Improper Credential Usage

This is the most critical mobile risk. Developers often mistakenly hardcode sensitive data directly into the application source code (e.g., AWS IAM keys, API tokens, backend database passwords). Attackers decompile the APK/IPA and simply search for strings matching key formats. Another vector is improperly utilizing biometric frameworks, checking authentication locally rather than relying on a secure backend token.

M2: Insecure Data Storage

Assuming the mobile device provides physical security is a fatal flaw. Storing unencrypted PII, session tokens, or passwords in Android's SharedPreferences (stored at /data/data/com.app.name/shared_prefs/), local SQLite databases, or iOS Plist files allows trivial extraction if the device is lost and rooted. Secure development mandates the use of Android Keystore or iOS Keychain for cryptographic key management and encrypted local storage.

topics.title: OWASP Mobile Top 10: Insecure Communication
topics.content:
Transport Layer Protection

While most apps use HTTPS/TLS, simply encrypting the channel is insufficient. The primary vulnerability is the failure to implement Certificate Pinning. Without pinning, the app trusts any certificate signed by a Certificate Authority (CA) located in the device's trust store. An attacker can use MDM, social engineering, or physical access to install a rogue Root CA certificate on the device. Tools like Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP can then intercept, decrypt, and modify all API traffic in a Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attack.

Certificate Pinning

Pinning hardcodes the hash of the server's public key (or the leaf certificate) directly into the mobile app. The app will only communicate with a server possessing that exact cryptographic identity, completely thwarting MitM attacks even if a rogue Root CA is installed.

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Mobile App Analysis
topics.content:
Static Analysis

Static analysis examines the application without running it. For Android, testers use apktool to reverse the APK, decode the AndroidManifest.xml (looking for over-permissive exported Activities, Receivers, or debuggable flags), and extract resources. Jadx or dex2jar converts the Dalvik bytecode (classes.dex) back into readable Java/Kotlin source code to hunt for logic flaws and hardcoded secrets.

Dynamic Analysis

Dynamic analysis involves running the app on a physical device or emulator (like Genymotion or Corellium) to observe its behavior, network traffic, and filesystem interactions. Testers look for insecure local storage writes during runtime and intercept API calls to test the backend infrastructure for web vulnerabilities like IDOR or SQLi.

topics.title: Dynamic Instrumentation with Frida
topics.content:
The Power of Frida

Frida is the premier dynamic instrumentation toolkit for penetration testers. It allows the injection of JavaScript snippets or custom libraries into native apps on Android, iOS, Windows, and macOS during runtime. It operates in user-space and does not require modifying or repackaging the target binary.

Frida Capabilities

  • Method Hooking: Intercepting function calls. An attacker can read the arguments passed to a function, modify them, or alter the return value. For example, hooking isPremiumUser() to always return true.
  • Bypassing Root Detection: Hooking the methods an app uses to check for root (e.g., checking for the su binary or Magisk) and forcing them to return negative.
  • Bypassing Certificate Pinning: The most common use case. By hooking the Android TrustManager or iOS SSL context, Frida can disable the pinning checks, routing traffic cleanly through Burp Suite.

topics.title: Advanced Mobile Malware Vectors
topics.content:
Repackaging and Sideloading

Attackers download legitimate apps, decompile them with apktool, inject a malicious payload (like a reverse shell or spyware), rebuild the APK, and sign it with their own key. They then distribute this trojanized app via third-party app stores or phishing links, relying on users enabling "Install from Unknown Sources" (sideloading).

Dynamic Code Loading (DCL)

To evade Google Play Protect and static analysis, sophisticated malware uses Android's DexClassLoader. The app submitted to the store is entirely benign. Once installed, it downloads a secondary, encrypted DEX file from an attacker-controlled server, decrypts it in memory, and executes the malicious code dynamically. This renders pre-installation static analysis useless.

modules.title: IoT and OT Hacking
modules.description: Exhaustive breakdown of Internet of Things (IoT) protocols, Operational Technology (OT) and SCADA systems, hardware interfaces, industrial control security vulnerabilities, and network segmentation strategies.
objectives: Deconstruct IoT architecture and communication protocols like MQTT, CoAP, and Zigbee.
Analyze IoT attack surfaces including default credentials, insecure firmware, and hardware interfaces (JTAG/UART).
Understand OT and ICS components (PLCs, RTUs, HMIs) and the Purdue Model for segmentation.
Exploit the lack of security in industrial protocols like Modbus TCP and analyze ICS malware like Stuxnet.

topics.title: IoT Architecture and Layers
topics.content:
The Three-Tier IoT Architecture

The Internet of Things connects physical objects to the digital world. It is generally modeled in three layers:

  • Edge/Perception Layer: The physical devices, sensors, and actuators that collect data from the environment (e.g., a smart thermostat reading temperature) and physical interactions.
  • Network/Transport Layer: The gateways and communication protocols that transmit data from the Edge to the Cloud. This includes wireless protocols (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Zigbee) and messaging protocols.
  • Application/Cloud Layer: The backend infrastructure where data is aggregated, processed, analyzed, and stored. It provides the user interface (mobile apps, web dashboards) for controlling the devices.

topics.title: IoT Communication Protocols: MQTT and CoAP
topics.content:
MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)

MQTT is the dominant IoT protocol due to its minimal footprint and low bandwidth requirements. It operates on a Publish/Subscribe model. Devices do not connect directly to each other; they connect to a central Broker. A sensor publishes data to a specific Topic (e.g., home/livingroom/temp). Other devices subscribe to that topic to receive updates. Security Flaw: If the broker lacks authentication and encryption (TLS), an attacker can subscribe to the wildcard topic (#), intercepting all telemetry data globally, or inject malicious commands.

CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)

CoAP is designed for constrained nodes (microcontrollers with ~100KB RAM) and low-power WANs. It translates standard HTTP semantics (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) into a compact, UDP-based binary protocol. Since UDP lacks inherent security, CoAP relies on DTLS (Datagram Transport Layer Security) for encryption and authentication. Improper DTLS implementation is the primary attack vector.

topics.title: IoT Wireless Protocols: Zigbee and Z-Wave
topics.content:
Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4)

Zigbee is a low-power, short-range protocol designed for mesh networking, commonly used in smart home automation (lighting, locks). Devices relay data for each other, extending the network's range. Zigbee relies on a Network Key (AES-128) for encryption. The primary vulnerability is the initial pairing process; if the Network Key is sniffed over the air while a new device joins, the entire mesh is compromised.

Z-Wave

Z-Wave is a proprietary (now open standard) protocol operating in the sub-1GHz frequency band (typically 900 MHz in the US). This lower frequency provides better penetration through walls compared to 2.4 GHz protocols like Zigbee or Wi-Fi, and avoids interference with them. Similar to Zigbee, it uses mesh networking and relies on secure key exchange during device inclusion.

topics.title: The IoT Attack Surface
topics.content:
Default and Hardcoded Credentials

The single most exploited vulnerability in IoT history. Devices (IP cameras, routers, DVRs) are shipped with default administrative credentials (e.g., admin/admin, root/12345) that users are never forced to change. The Mirai Botnet (2016) compromised millions of devices simply by scanning the internet for port 23 (Telnet) and attempting a short list of 61 default passwords, subsequently using them to launch the largest DDoS attacks in history.

Insecure Firmware and Updates

Many IoT devices pull firmware updates over unencrypted HTTP and fail to verify cryptographic signatures. An attacker performing a Man-in-the-Middle attack can intercept the update request and serve a maliciously modified firmware image. Once flashed, the device is permanently backdoored at the OS level.

topics.title: IoT Hardware Exploitation
topics.content:
Hardware Debugging Interfaces

When attackers gain physical access to an IoT device, they target the printed circuit board (PCB) to interface directly with the microcontroller and memory.

  • UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter): A serial communication protocol. Attackers connect a USB-to-TTL adapter to the Tx/Rx pins to access the device's boot log. Often, interrupting the boot sequence via UART drops the attacker into an unauthenticated root shell.
  • JTAG (Joint Test Action Group): An industry-standard interface for hardware debugging. Attackers use JTAG to halt the CPU, read/write directly to registers, and step through instructions, bypassing all software security.
  • SPI/I2C: Protocols used to communicate with flash memory chips. Attackers use tools like an SPI programmer to dump the entire firmware image directly from the EEPROM chip.

Firmware Reverse Engineering

Once dumped, firmware is analyzed using Binwalk, a tool that searches the binary file for known signatures (like squashfs file systems, compressed kernels, or SSL certificates). Extracting the filesystem allows attackers to reverse engineer proprietary binaries, find hidden API keys, or discover zero-day vulnerabilities in the web interface.

topics.title: OT, ICS, and SCADA Concepts
topics.content:
Operational Technology (OT)

OT encompasses the hardware and software that detects or causes a change through the direct monitoring and/or control of physical devices, processes, and events in the enterprise. While IT manages data, OT manages physical reality (manufacturing, power grids, water treatment).

ICS and SCADA Architecture

  • ICS (Industrial Control Systems): The overarching term for systems used to control industrial processes.
  • SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition): A subset of ICS used for large-scale, geographically dispersed operations (like a transcontinental pipeline). It centralizes data acquisition and supervisory control.
  • PLC (Programmable Logic Controller): Ruggedized, solid-state industrial computers that continuously monitor the state of input devices (sensors) and make decisions based on a custom program (Ladder Logic) to control the state of output devices (motors, valves).
  • HMI (Human-Machine Interface): The graphical dashboard where human operators monitor the process data and interact with the PLCs.

topics.title: The Purdue Model and Network Segmentation
topics.content:
The Purdue Enterprise Reference Architecture

The Purdue Model is the foundational framework for ICS network security. It mandates strict hierarchical segmentation to prevent threats in the corporate IT network from bleeding into the critical OT network.

  • Level 5 & 4 (Enterprise Network): Corporate IT, email, ERP systems. High risk of internet-borne threats (phishing, ransomware).
  • Level 3.5 (Industrial Demilitarized Zone - IDMZ): The critical buffer zone. Contains jump servers, patch management, and data historians. Firewalls strictly regulate traffic traversing this layer.
  • Level 3 (Site Manufacturing Operations): OT management systems, centralized engineering workstations.
  • Level 2 (Control Systems): Supervisory HMIs and SCADA software.
  • Level 1 (Basic Control): The PLCs and RTUs executing control logic.
  • Level 0 (Process): The physical sensors, actuators, and motors.

Without the Purdue Model (or equivalent segmentation), a ransomware infection on a corporate HR laptop can spread laterally directly to the PLCs, causing physical damage or catastrophic operational downtime.

topics.title: Industrial Protocols and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
OT vs. IT Priorities

In IT, the priority is the CIA triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability). In OT, the absolute priorities are Safety and Availability. Confidentiality is secondary. Security controls cannot introduce latency that might cause a physical process (like a centrifuge) to fail.

Modbus TCP

Designed in 1979 for serial communication and later adapted for TCP/IP (Port 502), Modbus is ubiquitous in ICS. It is a master/slave (client/server) protocol. Crucially, Modbus TCP completely lacks authentication and encryption. It inherently trusts all traffic on the network. Any device capable of routing packets to the PLC can issue Modbus read/write commands. An attacker can trivially send a command to alter a coil (turn a valve on/off) or overwrite a holding register (change a temperature threshold), manipulating the physical process.

Other Protocols

Similar vulnerabilities exist in DNP3 (used in utilities), IEC 104, and Siemens S7. Modern OT security relies on deep packet inspection (DPI) firewalls and anomaly detection to identify malicious commands within these cleartext protocols.

topics.title: ICS Malware Deep Dive: Stuxnet
topics.content:
The Stuxnet Worm (2010)

Stuxnet is the watershed moment in cyber warfare—the first malware specifically designed to cause physical destruction. It targeted the Iranian nuclear enrichment facility at Natanz.

Stuxnet's Attack Chain

  1. Infection: Bridged the air gap via infected USB drives, utilizing multiple zero-day exploits (including LNK shortcut execution and print spooler vulnerabilities) to propagate through the Windows IT network.
  2. Targeting: Looked specifically for Siemens Step7 software installed on engineering workstations.
  3. Sabotage: Once it found the target PLCs, it modified the underlying ladder logic (using a rootkit to hide the changes from the engineers). It altered the frequency of the variable-frequency drives, causing the uranium centrifuges to spin too fast and tear themselves apart.
  4. Deception: Crucially, while destroying the centrifuges, Stuxnet intercepted sensor data and played back normal operating telemetry to the HMI, making operators believe everything was functioning perfectly until physical destruction occurred.

modules.title: Cloud Computing and Applied Cryptography
modules.description: Deep dive into Cloud Security Posture Management, Serverless and Container security, alongside an exhaustive curriculum on fundamental and applied Cryptography.
objectives: Identify and exploit cloud misconfigurations, specifically focusing on IAM and exposed storage (S3/Blobs).
Analyze container (Docker/K8s) escape techniques and serverless architectures.
Master the mechanics of Symmetric (AES) and Asymmetric (RSA, ECC) encryption algorithms.
Evaluate Public Key Infrastructure (PKI), digital signatures, and hashing concepts.

topics.title: Cloud Service and Deployment Models
topics.content:
The Cloud Paradigm

Cloud computing abstracts physical hardware, delivering resources on-demand. The primary service models dictate the division of administrative control:

  • IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service): Provider supplies VMs, storage, and networking (e.g., AWS EC2). Customer manages OS, middleware, and data.
  • PaaS (Platform as a Service): Provider supplies the runtime and OS (e.g., Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk). Customer deploys code and data.
  • SaaS (Software as a Service): Provider manages the entire stack (e.g., Salesforce, Google Workspace). Customer only configures user access.

The Shared Responsibility Model

Security is a shared burden. The Cloud Service Provider (CSP) is responsible for 'Security of the Cloud' (physical datacenters, hypervisor patching). The customer is responsible for 'Security in the Cloud' (IAM policies, data encryption, firewall configurations). The vast majority of cloud breaches stem from customer misconfigurations, not CSP flaws.

topics.title: Identity and Access Management (IAM)
topics.content:
IAM: The New Perimeter

In the cloud, traditional network boundaries vanish. IAM (users, groups, roles, and policies) becomes the primary defense. The core philosophy must be the Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP).

IAM Misconfigurations and Privilege Escalation

Cloud environments utilize complex JSON-based policies. A common critical error is granting wildcard permissions, such as {"Effect": "Allow", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "*"}, which grants total control over all storage. If an attacker compromises an EC2 instance that has an overly permissive IAM Role attached, they can extract the temporary security credentials and assume that role, escalating privileges laterally across the entire cloud environment.

topics.title: SSRF and the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS)
topics.content:
Server-Side Request Forgery in the Cloud

SSRF is catastrophic in cloud environments. Every cloud provider exposes an internal, non-routable endpoint called the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS), typically at 169.254.169.254. This service provides configuration data to the VM.

The Attack Vector

If a web application hosted on an EC2 instance is vulnerable to SSRF (e.g., it fetches URLs provided by the user without validation), an attacker can force the server to query the IMDS endpoint. By requesting latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/, the IMDS returns the temporary IAM Access Key, Secret Key, and Session Token assigned to that instance. The attacker uses these keys on their local machine via the AWS CLI to impersonate the server. AWS introduced IMDSv2, which mitigates this by requiring a cryptographic session token for metadata requests.

topics.title: Cloud Storage Exposures
topics.content:
S3 Bucket Misconfigurations

Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service) and Azure Blob Storage hold massive amounts of enterprise data. Misconfiguring access controls is the leading cause of data leaks. If a bucket's Access Control List (ACL) or Bucket Policy is set to 'public-read' or allows the 'All Users'/`AuthenticatedUsers` group (which means any AWS user globally, not just in the corporate account), anyone can list and download the contents.

Exploitation

Attackers use automated tools to brute-force bucket names based on company profiles. Once an open bucket is found, they download backups, source code, or PII. Mitigations include enforcing "Block Public Access" at the account level and using Macie for data discovery.

topics.title: Container Security and Escape Techniques
topics.content:
Containerization (Docker)

Containers package applications and dependencies, sharing the host OS kernel via Linux Namespaces (for isolation) and cgroups (for resource limits). This makes them lightweight but presents unique security challenges compared to hardware-virtualized VMs.

Container Escapes

A container escape occurs when an attacker breaks out of the container's isolation to access the underlying host node. Common vectors include:

  • Privileged Containers: Running docker with --privileged disables crucial security mechanisms (like AppArmor/Seccomp) and grants the container full access to host devices, making escape trivial.
  • Docker Socket Mounting: If /var/run/docker.sock is mounted inside a container, a compromised container can use the socket to spawn new, fully privileged containers on the host, effectively taking over the node.
  • Kernel Exploits: Since containers share the host kernel, a local kernel privilege escalation exploit (like Dirty COW) executed inside the container compromises the entire host.

topics.title: Kubernetes and Serverless Security
topics.content:
Kubernetes (K8s) Security

K8s orchestrates clusters of containers (Pods). Security must span the Control Plane (API server, etcd) and the Data Plane (Kubelets). Misconfigurations include allowing unauthenticated access to the Kubelet API port, granting overly permissive RBAC roles, or failing to use Network Policies, which allows a compromised Pod to move laterally to any other Pod in the cluster.

Serverless (FaaS) Risks

Function as a Service (e.g., AWS Lambda) abstracts the server entirely. While the CSP handles OS patching, the attack surface shifts to the application code. Insecure deserialization, command injection, and over-privileged execution roles are the primary vectors. Because functions are ephemeral, traditional endpoint detection (EDR) is often ineffective.

topics.title: Cryptography: Core Concepts and Symmetric Encryption
topics.content:
The Foundation of Cryptography

Cryptography ensures Confidentiality (encryption), Integrity (hashing), Authentication (digital certificates), and Non-repudiation (digital signatures). Kerckhoffs's Principle states that a cryptographic system should be secure even if everything about the system, except the key, is public knowledge. "Security through obscurity" is a fallacy.

Symmetric Encryption

Uses a single, shared secret key for both encryption and decryption. It is fast and efficient for bulk data. The main drawback is the Key Distribution Problem—securely sharing the key over an untrusted network.

  • AES (Advanced Encryption Standard): The global standard. A block cipher (encrypts in 128-bit blocks) using keys of 128, 192, or 256 bits. Replaced the obsolete DES (56-bit key).
  • Cipher Modes: ECB (Electronic Codebook) is weak as identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks. CBC (Cipher Block Chaining) XORs each block with the previous one, requiring an IV. GCM (Galois/Counter Mode) is the modern standard, providing both encryption and authenticated integrity (AEAD).

topics.title: Asymmetric Encryption and PKI
topics.content:
Asymmetric (Public-Key) Cryptography

Uses a mathematically linked key pair: a Public Key (freely distributed) and a Private Key (kept secret). Data encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted by the private key. It solves the key distribution problem but is computationally expensive, so it's typically used to securely exchange a symmetric key, which then encrypts the actual data.

  • RSA: Security relies on the extreme difficulty of factoring the product of two very large prime numbers.
  • ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography): Uses the algebraic structure of elliptic curves. Provides equivalent security to RSA but with much smaller key sizes (e.g., a 256-bit ECC key is as strong as a 3072-bit RSA key), making it ideal for mobile and IoT devices.

Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)

PKI manages digital certificates. A Certificate Authority (CA) cryptographically signs a digital certificate, binding a Public Key to a specific entity (like a web domain). This establishes trust on the internet (HTTPS).

topics.title: Hashing, Signatures, and Integrity
topics.content:
Cryptographic Hash Functions

A hash function takes an input of any size and produces a fixed-size string (digest). It is one-way (irreversible). The Avalanche Effect dictates that changing a single bit of input dramatically changes the output hash. Used for verifying file integrity and storing passwords.

  • Algorithms: MD5 (Broken, collisions exist), SHA-1 (Deprecated), SHA-256 (Current standard).

Password Hashing and Salting

Passwords must be hashed, not encrypted. To thwart Rainbow Table attacks (precomputed hash databases), a unique random value called a Salt is appended to the password before hashing. Modern implementations use key derivation functions like bcrypt or PBKDF2, which introduce a computationally expensive "work factor" to slow down brute-force attacks.

Digital Signatures

Provide authentication and non-repudiation. A sender hashes a document, then encrypts that hash using their Private Key. The recipient decrypts the signature using the sender's Public Key. If the decrypted hash matches a newly computed hash of the document, it proves the sender authored it and the document was not altered.

modules.title: Advanced Cryptography and OT Deep Dive
modules.description: Exhaustive exploration of advanced cryptographic protocols, key exchange mechanisms, cryptanalysis models, quantum threats, alongside a deeper analysis of Operational Technology defense strategies.
objectives: Analyze key exchange protocols including Diffie-Hellman and the concept of Perfect Forward Secrecy.
Evaluate cryptanalysis attack models (Chosen-Plaintext, Known-Plaintext) and advanced concepts like Homomorphic Encryption.
Understand the threat of Quantum Computing to modern cryptography and Post-Quantum mitigations.
Deepen knowledge of ICS/OT defense mechanisms, threat frameworks, and secure architecture designs.

topics.title: Key Exchange and Forward Secrecy
topics.content:
Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (DH)

Diffie-Hellman allows two parties who have no prior knowledge of each other to jointly establish a shared secret key over an insecure channel. This key is then used to encrypt subsequent communications using a symmetric cipher. It relies on the mathematical difficulty of discrete logarithms. While it securely exchanges keys, DH does not provide authentication on its own, making it vulnerable to Man-in-the-Middle attacks unless combined with digital signatures (e.g., RSA).

Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)

PFS is a feature of specific key agreement protocols (like Ephemeral Diffie-Hellman, DHE or ECDHE). It ensures that your session keys will not be compromised even if the server's long-term private key is compromised in the future. With PFS, a unique session key is generated for every single transaction. If an attacker records years of encrypted traffic and eventually steals the server's RSA private key, they cannot decrypt the historical traffic because the private key was only used for authentication, not to derive the session keys.

topics.title: Cryptanalysis Attack Models
topics.content:
Understanding Cryptanalysis

Cryptanalysis is the study of breaking cryptographic systems to recover the plaintext or the key without knowing the key initially. Attacks are classified based on what information the cryptanalyst has access to.

  • Ciphertext-Only Attack: The hardest attack model. The attacker only has access to a set of ciphertexts. They rely on statistical analysis, frequency analysis (in classical ciphers), or brute force to find patterns.
  • Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA): The attacker possesses one or more pairs of known plaintext and their corresponding ciphertext. They use this to deduce the key used to encrypt the rest of the messages. (e.g., knowing a file always starts with a specific header).
  • Chosen-Plaintext Attack (CPA): The attacker has the ability to choose arbitrary plaintexts to be encrypted and obtain the corresponding ciphertexts. This allows them to analyze how the algorithm processes specific inputs, revealing mathematical weaknesses.
  • Chosen-Ciphertext Attack (CCA): The attacker can choose different ciphertexts to be decrypted and obtain the corresponding plaintexts.

topics.title: Advanced Cryptographic Concepts
topics.content:
Homomorphic Encryption

Traditional encryption requires data to be decrypted before it can be processed (e.g., in a database query). This exposes the data in memory. Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) is a revolutionary cryptographic scheme that allows mathematical and logical operations to be performed directly on ciphertext. The result of these operations, when decrypted, matches the result as if the operations were performed on the plaintext. This allows cloud providers to process highly sensitive data (like medical records or financial models) without ever seeing the unencrypted data. It is currently highly resource-intensive but represents the future of data privacy.

Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP)

ZKP is a cryptographic method by which one party (the prover) can prove to another party (the verifier) that a given statement is true, without conveying any information apart from the fact that the statement is indeed true. For example, proving you are over 18 without revealing your actual date of birth. Used heavily in modern privacy-focused cryptocurrencies and advanced authentication schemes.

topics.title: The Quantum Computing Threat and PQC
topics.content:
The Quantum Threat

Quantum computers leverage the principles of quantum mechanics (superposition and entanglement) to perform certain calculations exponentially faster than classical computers. Shor's Algorithm, when run on a sufficiently powerful quantum computer, can easily factor large prime numbers and solve discrete logarithms. This means that nearly all modern public-key cryptography—specifically RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)—will be fundamentally broken. Symmetric algorithms like AES-256 and hash functions like SHA-256 are largely resistant to quantum attacks (though Grover's algorithm halves their effective key size, AES-256 remains secure).

Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)

To prepare for "Q-Day" (the day quantum computers can break RSA), organizations like NIST are standardizing PQC algorithms. These algorithms run on classical computers but are based on mathematical problems that are currently believed to be unsolvable by both classical and quantum computers, such as Lattice-based cryptography (e.g., ML-KEM / CRYSTALS-Kyber) and Hash-based cryptography.

topics.title: Defending Operational Technology (OT)
topics.content:
Hardening the Purdue Model

While network segmentation via the Purdue Model is foundational, advanced OT environments require more robust physical and logical controls due to the catastrophic consequences of a breach.

  • Unidirectional Gateways (Data Diodes): In highly critical environments (like nuclear power), firewalls are insufficient because they can be misconfigured or exploited. A Data Diode is a physical hardware device that only allows data to flow in one direction (e.g., from the OT network to the IT network for logging/monitoring). It is physically impossible for traffic to travel back into the OT network, eliminating remote command injection attacks.
  • Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) for ICS: Traditional IT firewalls look at ports and IPs. OT firewalls must understand industrial protocols (Modbus, DNP3, CIP). DPI allows the firewall to inspect the payload of a Modbus packet and block specific function codes (e.g., allowing 'Read Register' commands but blocking 'Write Register' commands from unauthorized engineering workstations).
  • ICS Threat Intelligence: Utilizing frameworks like MITRE ATT&CK for ICS to understand the specific tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) used by nation-state actors targeting critical infrastructure, such as manipulating control logic (T833) or spoofing reporting messages (T856).
